references.title: Genetic Algorithms for Optimizing Grouping of Students Classmates in Engineering Education
references.journal: International Journal of Information and Education Technology

annotations.text: Use of genetic algorithms to group students of engineering. There have been previous utilizations of such a methodology to create groups in lab assignments or other parts involving collaborative learning.

Authors evaluate the impact of their approach about the impact on collaborative performance, processes and perceptions. REsults show that there is a positive impact and that students tend to see this approach as “fair”.

Student grouping should incorporate a multi-variate and multi-faceted approach. Students have their individual technical capabilities but other traits such as motivation to learn and/or character development should also be considered. This brings us into 2 distinct forms of grouping students:

It is important to have good classes in order to avoid situations where the best students are all bunched up into a class or another that cannot progress as well as others because it lacks “someone at the level” which can make the gaps between different classes become increasingly larger. The authors then transition into associating genetic algorithms with the constructivist perspective of learning (which I won’t deny, is definitely odd). Regardless, the paper aims to use genetic algorithms to create distinct classes that are:
Explanation of what GAs are follows:

Objective of the paper is to make better classes with GA. Better classes in this context refers to creating classes that enhance academic achievement, promote participation and engagement and foster diversity within groups.

Definition of population: students will be selected from a random study programme - in this case Computer Engineering with 114 students. Students will be classified according to 11 criteria: Entrance, School of Origin, Gender, IPA value, IPS score, Language Score, Math Score, GPA, hometown, religion and Parent’s Income.

Genetic Algorithm: Big initial blunder mentioning that Darwin discovered Genetic Algorithms. But in general, Genetic algorithms have different individuals be a potential solution and these individuals are continuously crossed with one another until we get new individuals and new individuals. Each individual then represents a potential solution.

Other studies have already used GAs in education. Moreno used them to create well-balanced student groups according to academic performance. The fitness function for that test was an ANOVA - a statistical method meant to compare means between groups. The findings suggest that GA was better than the remaining methods at generating wel-balanced groups ( so, minimizing between group differences??).
So, how does this genetic algorithm work?

Generate 100 random individuals. Each gene refers to the criteria possessed by the students individidual that is part of the class whereas each chromossome refers to the combination of individuals to be used - lenght of 16.
At this stage the authors compute the fitness values of each chromossome. If criteria for optimality are met stop, otherwise form a new generation.

Fitness is going to be computed as a measure of diversity. The higher the diversity in fitness values, the “more fit a chromosome” is going to be. Thus, the fitness function can be understood as a “count of unique genes”.
Selection involves hill climbing - individuals with the highest fitness are kept.

The students were divided into 12 classes of 16 students each → problem formulation. These students are assigned randomly before the start. From this random assignment, the total fitness of this solution is 703.

The process, involves assigning a class code to each individual. Then, there is a score called ATT that is computed as a measure of uniqueness of the individual. The fitness function is the count of this ATT. Authors employ crossover to prevent premature convergence.

Final solution achieves a Fitness value of 701. When comparing this solution against that of a k-means clustering, the authors argue that there is a lot more diversity on the grouping done by genetic algorithms in comparison with the one obtained via k-means (I’ll be honest, I don’t understand how you can look at these graphs and conclude anything, but ok).
My guess is that the bars on the GA solution are not as “even” means that there is more in-class variability??? Is that it?
Findings “demonstrate - very loose term” that genetic algortihms are an effective way of producing diverse groups of students - thus promoting inclusive learning practices.
